title: Lokeśvarastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; likely 1st-2nd millennium CE, transmitted in Nepalese Buddhist liturgy (collection compiled by the medieval/early-modern period)
summary: A devotional hymn praising Lokeśvara, the 'Lord of the World,' an epithet of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, the embodiment of compassion. It extols his salvific power and is recited for protection and blessing.
history: Preserved among the Buddhist Sanskrit stotras of the Kathmandu Valley and printed in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994), where it appears anonymously. Such hymns remain in living recitation use in Newar Buddhism.

title: Narakoddhārastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern transmission within Nepalese Buddhist hymn corpus
summary: A hymn on 'rescue from the hells' (naraka-uddhāra), invoking a Buddha or bodhisattva (typically Avalokiteśvara) as a savior who lifts beings out of hellish rebirths and the suffering of the lower realms.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha collection of 108 Buddhist stotras edited by J. S. Pandey (Varanasi, 1994); part of the living Newar Buddhist devotional repertoire of the Kathmandu Valley.

title: Potalakāṣṭaka
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) associated with Potalaka, the mountain abode of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, praising the deity of that sacred site. It celebrates Avalokiteśvara/Lokeśvara in his Potala aspect.
history: Anonymous within the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Belongs to the Avalokiteśvara-centred devotional literature preserved in Nepalese Buddhism.

title: Rakṣākāla(kara)stava
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn of praise to a protective deity (rakṣā, 'protection'), invoked for warding off danger and harm. The title variant Rakṣākāra/Rakṣākāla refers to one who effects protection.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha of 108 Buddhist stotras (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Such apotropaic hymns are part of the protective (rakṣā) ritual literature of Newar Buddhism.

title: Ratnamālāstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A 'Jewel-Garland' hymn praising a Buddha or bodhisattva, the verses likened to a string of jewels offered in devotion. It is a praise-poem in the Mahayana devotional mode.
history: Anonymous within the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994), preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist hymn tradition.

title: Rūpastava
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn praising the bodily form (rūpa) of a Buddha or bodhisattva, dwelling on the perfected, auspicious physical appearance and major and minor marks of the divine figure as an object of devotion.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha collection of 108 Buddhist stotras (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); part of the Newar Buddhist devotional corpus.

title: Ṣaḍgatistotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A short hymn on the 'six destinies' (ṣaḍgati) of Buddhist cosmology — the realms of gods, demigods, humans, animals, hungry ghosts, and hell-beings — likely invoking a savior bodhisattva who liberates beings from cyclic rebirth across these six realms.
history: A six-verse anonymous hymn in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994), reflecting the doctrine of the six realms of saṃsāra in Mahayana devotional practice.

title: Saptākṣarastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn built around the 'seven syllables' (saptākṣara), most likely the seven-syllable mantra/form associated with a tantric deity (such as Heruka/Saṃvara in the saptākṣara form). It praises the deity through the power of the mantric syllables.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The saptākṣara theme links it to the mantra-based hymnody of Newar Vajrayana.

title: Saptavidhānuttarastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A twelve-verse hymn on the 'sevenfold supreme worship' (saptavidha anuttarā pūjā) — the standard sevenfold devotional service (homage, offering, confession of faults, rejoicing in merit, requesting teaching, requesting the buddhas to remain, and dedication of merit) central to Mahayana liturgy.
history: Anonymous hymn (12 verses) in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The sevenfold worship (saptāṅga/saptavidhā pūjā) derives from Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra and pervades later devotional practice.

title: Śāradāṣṭakastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) to Śāradā, an aspect of the goddess of learning and eloquence (Sarasvatī/Prajñā), praising her as the bestower of wisdom and speech.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Sarasvatī/Śāradā is venerated in Buddhist as well as Hindu traditions; this hymn belongs to the Newar Buddhist devotional corpus.

title: Vāgvāṇīstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn to Vāgvāṇī ('the Voice/Word'), an epithet of Sarasvatī, the goddess of speech, wisdom, and learning. It praises her as the source of eloquence and knowledge.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); part of the Sarasvatī devotional hymnody shared between Hindu and Newar Buddhist worship.

title: Daśabhūmīśvaro nāma mahāyānasūtraratnarājastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn titled 'Lord of the Ten Stages, the Jewel-King among Mahayana sutras,' praising the bodhisattva path through the ten bhūmis (stages) and likely eulogizing the Daśabhūmika-sūtra itself as the 'jewel-king' of Mahayana scriptures.
history: Anonymous hymn in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Its theme draws on the Daśabhūmika-sūtra, the foundational text of the ten bodhisattva stages later incorporated into the Avataṃsaka.

title: Caityavandanāstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn of homage (vandanā) to the caitya/stūpa, the reliquary shrine that symbolizes the Buddha. It expresses veneration of the stupa as an object of worship and a focus of merit-making.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Caitya/stūpa veneration is a central practice of Newar Buddhism, where the hymn would accompany circumambulation and offering.

title: Vidyākṣarastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
summary: A hymn structured on the 'syllables of knowledge/spell' (vidyākṣara), praising a deity through the letters of a vidyā (mantra/spell). Each syllable is correlated with an aspect of the deity or a soteriological power.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); belongs to the akṣara-based mantric hymnody of Newar Vajrayana.

title: Cakrasaṃvarastuti
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult (tantra itself c. 8th-9th c. CE; this hymn later)
summary: A hymn of praise to Cakrasaṃvara (Heruka), the principal yidam of the Cakrasaṃvara/Saṃvara tantric system, depicted in union with his consort Vajravārāhī. It extols his fierce iconography and the maṇḍala over which he presides.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The Cakrasaṃvara tradition is centrally maintained by the Newar Buddhists of the Kathmandu Valley, where such stutis form part of sādhana practice.

title: Catuḥṣaṣṭisaṃvarastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
summary: A hymn praising the sixty-four (catuḥṣaṣṭi) deities of the Saṃvara maṇḍala, enumerating and venerating the retinue of yoginīs, ḍākas, and ḍākinīs surrounding Cakrasaṃvara and Vajravārāhī.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The number 64 reflects the goddesses/deities of the Saṃvara maṇḍala system in the yoginītantra literature.

title: Pīṭhastava
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
summary: A hymn to the sacred seats or power-places (pīṭha) of the tantric geography — the network of pīṭhas, upapīṭhas and other sites associated with the Cakrasaṃvara/Vajravārāhī maṇḍala and the yoginī cult. It venerates these sites and their presiding deities.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The pīṭha scheme is a defining feature of the Cakrasaṃvara/yoginītantra system, mapped onto both external geography and the practitioner's body.

title: Ṣaṭtriṃśatsaṃvarastuti
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
summary: A hymn praising the thirty-six (ṣaṭtriṃśat) deities of a Saṃvara maṇḍala, eulogizing the central couple Cakrasaṃvara-Vajravārāhī together with their thirty-six-fold retinue of yoginīs and guardians.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Paired thematically with the Catuḥṣaṣṭisaṃvarastotra, it reflects the variant maṇḍala enumerations of the Saṃvara cycle.

title: Vasantatilakāstuti
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
summary: A hymn whose title puns on the Vasantatilakā metre and on Vasantatilakā as a name associated with the Heruka/Saṃvara cycle (the Abhidhānottara/Vasantatilaka tradition). It is a tantric eulogy connected to the Cakrasaṃvara-Vajravārāhī literature.
history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). 'Vasantatilaka' figures as a title within Heruka/Saṃvara tantric literature as well as being a classical Sanskrit metre.

title: Dharmadhātunāmastava
author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (attribution disputed by some modern scholars)
date_estimate: if Nāgārjuna, c. 2nd-3rd c. CE; attribution and dating disputed
summary: A hymn 'in praise of the Dharmadhātu' (the basic realm/element of all phenomena), in roughly 100 verses, describing how the luminous dharmadhātu (equated with buddha-nature) is obscured by adventitious defilements in ordinary beings, progressively uncovered along the bodhisattva path, and fully revealed as buddhahood.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna (the Dharmadhātustava/Dharmadhātustotra) and unquestioned in India and Tibet, though some modern scholars doubt the attribution given its tathāgatagarbha content. Long known only in Tibetan and Chinese, a Sanskrit manuscript surfaced and was published in 2015; here it appears within the Newar Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha.
